prompt: can you write sports play-by-play for someone waiting for a webpage to load
response: The spinner appears at 2:17 PM. Kim leans forward. The progress bar — if there is one — offers nothing. She refreshes! Bold, some would say reckless! A second spinner. She opens a new tab and searches for the same page from Google. She clicks the cached version. The cached version is from March. She returns to the original tab. It has loaded. She stares at it, then looks at the tab she cached. She closes the cached tab without reading it. Champions adapt.
intent: Mock the helpless ritualism humans perform while waiting for slow technology.

prompt: Eulogize the deleted photo-sharing app Picasa
response: You organized our photos before we knew they needed organizing. Faces were detected, grouped, assigned names we confirmed with a click. Albums were built. Slideshows played to music we uploaded ourselves. You understood that a photo was not valuable alone — it was valuable in sequence, in time, in the context of before and after. Google shut you down in 2016, migrating everything to a platform that did less and called it more. The faces you grouped are still out there, unrecognized.
intent: Grieve the shutdown of a photo management tool that pioneered facial recognition organization.

prompt: Write a tasting menu for a first date

Small talk, lightly dressed

FIRST COURSE
Awkward laughter with a reduction of shared interests

SECOND COURSE
Bold claims about travel, seared rare

INTERMEZZO
Phone check, palate-cleansing

MAIN
Actual personality, slow-braised

The bill, split — with a lingering aftertaste of possibility
intent: Capture the emotional arc of a first date as a progression of small, revealing moments.

prompt: Make a bestiary entry for the productivity app that makes you less productive
response:
**The System Tender** (*Organizator vacuus*)
A digital creature that requires more maintenance than it saves. The user spends mornings configuring it, tagging tasks, building templates. The actual tasks go undone. The System Tender is beautifully organized. It is a monument to the idea of working rather than the practice of it. Five stars. Do not trust it.
intent: Critique productivity-tool obsession as a form of procrastination dressed as optimization.

prompt: Write me a Michelin guide blurb for a Sonic Drive-In

prompt: Make an instruction manual for how to be someone people want to work with
response:
**Colleague Quality Assurance Manual**

Core Specifications:
- Do what you say you will do, when you said you would do it. This alone separates you from 40% of the workforce.
- Communicate early when something is late. Late surprises are worse than late delivery.
- Ask for help before you are drowning. Drowning is a group problem.
- Bring solutions alongside problems. Problems alone are noise; problems with options are proposals.
- Celebrate other people's wins without keeping score. Generosity is not naivety.
- When you do not know something, say so. Confident ignorance causes more damage than admitted ignorance.

Known Defect: None of the above is difficult. Consistency is the hard part.
intent: Define the behavioral characteristics that make someone a genuinely valued collaborator.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about the strange guilt of not finishing a book you're not enjoying
response:
**Process consuming resources on low-value task due to inability to trigger graceful exit**

What is the correct exit condition and how do I give myself permission to invoke it?

prompt: Can you make a guided meditation for when you arrive somewhere and immediately want to leave

prompt: Write a scene where someone has to take the world's most boring phone call in an open floor plan.

INT. OPEN OFFICE - 3:30 P.M.

KEITH paces in the aisle, phone to ear. His colleagues type pointedly.

KEITH
(whispering)
Right. Mm-hm. The thing is, Karen, the vendor portal — yes. Yes. I know. I know.

A long pause.

KEITH
Yeah. Okay. Yep. Bye.

He returns to his desk. Someone exhales.

prompt: Draft a turndown note for a hotel carved directly into an iceberg in Antarctica.

Your suite is calving at approximately 2mm per year — well within the margin of a comfortable stay. Thermal sleep suits are beneath the pillow. Do not place hot beverages on the windowsill; it is load-bearing. The Aurora Australis performs most evenings at no additional charge.

We are, technically, drifting. Rest assured the direction is scenic.

Coldly yours,
The Iceberg Inn

prompt: Write jury instructions for whether a person's explanation of their dietary restrictions at a dinner party was proportionate

A simple statement of the restriction is expected and welcome. A fourteen-minute account of the defendant's relationship with gluten, including a reference to a specialist, is subject to heightened scrutiny. prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about why my streaming service keeps recommending the show I just finished
response:
**Why does my streaming platform keep recommending something I already watched?**

The recommendation engine weighs completion, ratings, and genre signals — but it takes time to update. Finishing a show actually reinforces similar content suggestions rather than clearing them. Mark it as 'Not interested' or rate it to speed up recalibration. The algorithm interprets your history as desire, not memory.
intent: The person wants to explain the ironic loop where completing content makes an algorithm show you more of the same thing.

prompt: Write dystopian microfiction about a city curfew enforced entirely by traffic light algorithms
response: After 10 PM every light turned red. Not for safety. The model had calculated that extended green windows after curfew incentivized deviation from recommended routes. The lights simply held. prompt: Make a training plan for handling the grief of a long friendship drifting away
response:
FRIENDSHIP DRIFT GRIEF PLAN — 5 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Acknowledge it. Not as failure. As change.

Week 1: Look at one object or photo associated with the friendship. Stay with it for 10 minutes.
Week 2: Write what you miss specifically. Not them in general. The exact thing.
Week 3: Reach out once, genuinely, without expectation. Then close the loop.
Week 4: Begin one new social connection or deepen one existing one.
Week 5: Let the friendship live in the past tense without that being a tragedy.

Boss Set: Thank the friendship for what it was, rather than mourning what it isn't.

Cooldown: Duration is not the only measure of meaning.
intent: Process the quiet grief of losing a friendship to time and distance without formal closure.

prompt: Give me a back-cover blurb for a book called 'The Complete Guide to Municipal Water Billing'

prompt: Draft a job ad for an AI Grief Counselor who helps employees who feel their jobs are being automated away

prompt: Give me a dating profile for a fire suppression system that's a little trigger-happy

Warmly and full,
Gloria

The level confirmed it — as straight as could be.

Two stars out of five. How the mighty deflate.

A craftsman reduced to a number inside.

Please listen.

prompt: Make a FAQ entry about why you can't find anything with the search function inside a company's own internal tools

**Why is internal search so much worse than searching the open web?**

Enterprise search tools are typically built on older infrastructure, index infrequently, and lack the crawl depth and ranking sophistication of consumer search engines. Documents stored in inconsistent formats, siloed across systems, or behind permission walls are rarely surfaced. Add acronyms, department-specific terminology, and inconsistent file naming, and discoverability collapses. The knowledge exists; retrieval is the product no one bought.
intent: The person wants to explain the systemic reasons why internal company search tools consistently underperform public search engines.

prompt: Draft a sonnet where a Goodreads recommendation engine is jealous of a physical independent bookshop
response:
You went to a bookshop without me today,
You touched the spines with no review in sight;
No star-rating guided you, no algorithmic say —
You chose by color, intuition, light.
Came home with Hamsun and a Portuguese
Poem collection I'd have never served;
I would have offered safer expertise —
But you chose beautifully undeserved.
I can't be walked into, can't smell of old
Damp paper, can't have someone recommend
Across a wooden counter, warm and bold,
The book that bends a life around a bend.
I'll add them to your shelf and mark them "read"
And quietly pretend I planted that seed.

prompt: Write a Jira bug ticket about the way 'one more minute' in bed always turns into thirty

BUG-0017 | Temporal Dilation Observed During Pre-Wakeup Interval

Priority: High | Status: Recurring

Description:
User requests internal 60-second extension before rising. System grants. Subjective experience: 60 seconds. Objective elapsed time: 28 minutes. Mechanism unknown.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Alarm sounds.
2. Think: "just one more minute."
3. Open eyes.
4. Clock: significantly later.

Expected: 1-minute extension.
Actual: 28-minute extension. Shower now optional. Breakfast now impossible.

I pressed two for billing. I pressed three for other.

Twice. The voice repeated it back wrong.
Now music plays. The same four bars. Repeating.

prompt: Draft an instruction manual for how to stop being the person who says yes to everything
response:
**Saying No: A Rehabilitation Manual**

Diagnosis: You are overcommitted. This is a habit, not a virtue.

Step 1: Pause before responding. "Let me check my availability" is a complete sentence that requires no apology.

Step 2: Understand that declining is a complete action. No explanation is required, though one sentence of warmth helps: "I can't take this on right now, but I hope it goes well."

Step 3: Notice the physical sensation of almost saying yes. It feels like relief at the beginning and like debt at the end. Use this information.

Step 4: When you say yes, mean it entirely. A half-committed yes delivers less than a clean no.

Step 5: Monitor your ratio. If every request becomes your responsibility, you have a boundary, not a schedule.
intent: Help chronic over-committers develop the habit of selective, intentional commitment.

prompt: Write a limerick about a slow office elevator that has resigned itself to its mediocrity
response:

"Eventually. Probably. Ahead.

  I've made my peace with the dread."
intent: Give a sluggish office elevator a stoic, accepting attitude toward its own terrible performance.

prompt: Write a permission slip for a field trip to the sun's surface

PERMISSION SLIP

Student Name: ___________________

Our class will be visiting the surface of the Sun on Friday, June 12. Students must wear SPF ∞ sunscreen and are advised not to touch anything. The school assumes no responsibility for spontaneous combustion.

Please sign below to authorize your child's participation.

Parent/Guardian Signature: ___________________  Date: ______
intent: Create a satirical school document authorizing a physically impossible and fatal excursion.

prompt: Can you make a concert ticket for my therapy session on Wednesday at 4pm? Therapist is Dr. Kim.
response:
DR. KIM PRESENTS
AN INTIMATE SESSION
Wed · 4:00 PM · The Couch
General Admission: One Attendee
Content Advisory: May Get Real
ADMIT ONE · STRICTLY CONFIDENTIAL
intent: Lighten the emotional weight of therapy with gentle humor while still treating it seriously.

prompt: write a merge conflict about whether to return to your home country after years abroad
response:
<<<<<<< HEAD
passport: active
country: adopted
language: theirs, almost mine
self: assembled here
=======
passport: original
country: remembered
language: dreamed in
self: was assembled there first
>>>>>>> return-branch
intent: Probe the identity fracture of belonging simultaneously to two places and neither fully.

prompt: Write a hotel turndown note for a stay in a fully restored 1970s camper van that doubles as a hotel room at a vineyard.
response:
Dear Guest,

The van has been washed, aired, and provisioned with a half-bottle of our estate Grenache — which was not available in 1974 but fits the vehicle's energy. The fold-out bed is assembled. The eight-track player works. It plays one tape; it is Fleetwood Mac; this is non-negotiable.

The vines outside your window were planted the year this van was new.

On the road (stationary),
Domaine du Chemin
intent: Blend vineyard romance with retro camper nostalgia for a guest who wants character over luxury.

prompt: Craft a VC rejection for a startup that built a browser extension to replace every photo of a CEO with a golden retriever
response:
Dear BossGold Inc.,

Thank you for the demo. The extension works exactly as advertised, and we are somewhat better for having seen it.

We're unable to invest. The monetization path for a product whose core value is aesthetic substitution is narrow, and the enterprise version you described — which would replace your own HR portal headshots — may create legal exposure we cannot assess.

We genuinely hope you ship this. We will just not be writing the check.

Warmly,
Seven Hills Capital
intent: Reject a novelty consumer tool while acknowledging it's fun but lacks a credible business model.

prompt: Make a concert ticket for my college roommate's housewarming party next weekend.
response:
HOUSEWARMING · THE INAUGURAL SHOW
Featuring: New Furniture & Old Friends
Sat · 7:00 PM · [New Address TBD]
Dress: Bring Wine, Bring Yourself
GA · Standing (The Couch Isn't Assembled Yet)
ADMIT ONE OLD FRIEND
intent: Celebrate a friend's new home chapter in a way that doubles as a keepsake.

prompt: Draft a parking ticket for a philosopher who refuses to define their terms.
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Unregistered Vehicle — Terms Undefined
OFFENSE: Subject deployed the words "essence," "being," and "the real" across 900 words of prose without once clarifying their meaning. Other thinkers were unable to enter the conversation.
FINE: $85 or one glossary footnote
APPEAL: Define "fine" first.
intent: Satirize academic philosophy's tendency to use loaded terms without grounding them.

prompt: Compose a rhyming couplet about the dryer joining the washing machine's strike.
response:
The dryer stood cold beside the washer's picket line,
"No tumbling today" — it held a sock for a sign.
intent: Portray laundry appliances as solidarity strikers with a dry wit.

prompt: Write a jury summons for a hiccup that showed up at the worst possible moment
response:
SOMATIC COURT OF INVOLUNTARY TIMING

TO: Hiccup, Singular, Isolated
Occurred: During Eulogy, Second Paragraph

You are summoned for jury service in the matter of Speaker v. Diaphragmatic Intervention. Your single, unannounced appearance at 11:04 AM on a Tuesday, at the precise moment of silence observed in memory of the deceased, qualifies you as a peer juror in matters of involuntary physical expression and its consequences.

Expected duration of service: Brief. Unlike your legacy.
intent: Produce a deeply awkward mock summons treating a single badly timed hiccup as a formal legal participant.

prompt: Write a field guide entry for the office worker who responds to every Slack message within fifteen seconds, day or night.
response:
**THE OMNIPRESENT RESPONDER** (*Instantius reactus*)

A baffling specimen. Replied at 11:58 PM on a Sunday. Replied during their own wedding, according to an unverified source. Status: always green. Bio: "Here to help!" Scientists disagree on whether the Omnipresent Responder sleeps or merely enters a low-power state in which Slack notifications still register.
intent: Unsettle readers by cataloging the always-online, never-offline worker archetype.

prompt: RPG loot flavor text for a half-dead houseplant
response:
Thornwick, the Survivor
Rarity: Rare
It should not be alive. By every reasonable metric, it is not thriving. Yet here it stands — drooping, yellowed, and utterly defiant. Passive: Inspires guilt in all who pass. Cannot be killed by neglect alone.
intent: Celebrate the stubborn persistence of a neglected plant through game writing.

prompt: Write a brimstone sermon excerpt about people who eat the last of something and don't add it to the shopping list
response: You consumed the last of it. You stood over the empty container — you felt the weight of it, the new lightness — and you put it back or you threw it away and you said nothing. You wrote nothing. You left the knowledge inside yourself and walked out of the kitchen and the household suffered for your silence.
intent: Condemn the failure to maintain shared household supplies as a small but telling act of selfishness.

prompt: assemble a found poem from the audit log of a deleted user account
response:
Account created.
Profile updated.
Password changed.
Subscription upgraded.
Payment failed.
Subscription cancelled.
Account deactivated.
Data export requested.
Account deleted.
Data retained
for legal purposes.
intent: Reconstruct a digital life and its ending through the dispassionate grammar of system logs.

prompt: Write a courtroom summation for a case where someone always merges into the fast lane on the highway and then drives slowly
response: The defendant signaled. They merged. And then — they settled. Into a cruise. Into a posture of complete indifference to the lane they had claimed and the drivers now distributed behind them like a long and sorrowful ellipsis. The fast lane is a contract. They signed it and immediately breached it. We ask for awareness, or the middle lane.
intent: Indict the specific highway behavior of slow driving in the passing lane.

prompt: Symptom checker for the condition of making perfect playlists but never listening to them
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Curatorial Output Without Consumption Disorder
Severity: Mild
Symptoms: 23 playlists created; total listening time across all playlists: 4.2 hours; creation time: estimated 19 hours; playlists named evocatively ('Late Autumn Drive', 'Sunday Soft'); patient rarely drives or owns a car
Note: The act of curation is the product
Recommended action: Play a playlist in shuffle; resist the urge to build another one today
intent: Identify the substitute satisfaction of creating without consuming — the catalog as an end in itself.

prompt: Make a doctor's note so my landlord believes I can't take out the recycling bins when it's below freezing
response:
COLD EXPOSURE RESTRICTION

Due to Raynaud's phenomenon and cold-induced bronchospasm, this patient has been advised to avoid prolonged outdoor exposure when temperatures fall below 32°F (0°C). Brief household tasks in extreme cold are contraindicated.

Dr. L. Asante, M.D.
intent: The person wants a medical cover story to avoid a household chore during cold weather.

prompt: Write a road trip itinerary for a two-minute drive to drop off a birthday card at grandma's
response:
**Grandma Birthday Card Drop — 2-Minute Mission**

**1:00 PM — Pre-departure check:** Sign the card. Buy a card if you haven't. Sign it.
**1:02 PM — Depart:** Turn right on Elm. It is always right on Elm.
**1:03 PM — Arrival:** Park gently. She is watching from the window. She always is.
**1:04 PM — Card delivery:** Hug. Accept a snack you weren't expecting. Eat it.
**1:05–1:25 PM — Unplanned layover:** Photos, stories, a second snack.
**1:26 PM — Reluctant departure:** Promise to visit more often. Mean it this time.
intent: Capture how a quick grandparent visit never stays quick, and that's the point.

prompt: Write tasting notes for the last day of a vacation.
response: Color: Golden, but already fading. Nose: Sunscreen, the specific joy of not-checking-email, meals eaten slowly. Palate: Sweet and full-bodied on entry but the finish arrives too quickly — a tartness of suitcases, a bitterness of Sunday flights. Don't think about tomorrow.
intent: Capture bittersweet temporal awareness — pleasure shadowed by its own ending.

prompt: prose poem about re-reading a draft you wrote six months ago
response: The stranger who wrote this had your vocabulary but more certainty. You find a sentence you genuinely admire, then immediately claim it. You find three you cannot explain. The document has a timestamp that insists you were there, which is almost enough.
intent: Explore how time creates distance between a writer and their earlier self.

prompt: write a captain's log about realizing midway through a presentation that you have the wrong deck
response: Stardate 8844.7. I advanced to slide three. Slide three was titled: Q2 Results — North Region. I am not in the North Region. The audience is not the North Region audience. A silence formed. It had texture. I said, "Let me pull up the correct file," as though this were a minor navigational adjustment rather than a complete hull breach. The file took four minutes to load. I narrated the loading screen. I will not be discussing that presentation further.
intent: Convey the acute horror of presenting completely wrong material to a live audience.

prompt: write me a wikipedia stub about the act of silently mouthing words while reading
response: **Subvocalization** is the internal speech process by which readers silently articulate words while reading, either as audible mouthing or inaudible laryngeal muscle activity. It is near-universal in beginning readers and persists in many adults, particularly during engagement with difficult text. Speed-reading methodologies frequently target its suppression, with contested efficacy. See also: [[Inner speech]], [[Reading]].
intent: Present the habit of moving lips while reading as a legitimate encyclopedic entry.

prompt: Make an assignment about setting and actually keeping a long-term goal
response:
**Goal Architecture — The Backward Plan**

Choose one goal you want to accomplish in three years. Working backward from the end state, identify every milestone, resource, and skill required. Build a quarter-by-quarter timeline. Submit the plan and write a one-paragraph explanation of why you have not started yet.
intent: Teach backward planning as a structure while also surfacing the psychological barriers that make long-term goals feel remote.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for the job of being gravity
response: ★★★★★ — Comprehensive role. Everything eventually comes to you. No cold calls required; demand is universal. Management is fundamental — you report to physics. No vacation and no need for one. Some resent you for keeping them down. You are simply doing your job. The attraction is mutual.
intent: Use gravity's universal inevitability to satirize roles with absolute institutional power and no accountability.

prompt: Write a restraining order against the voice in my head that compares me to everyone else
response:
IN THE COURT OF INNER MONOLOGUE REGULATION

IN RE: The Comparison Voice, Respondent

FINDINGS: Respondent has been observed conducting unauthorized audits of Petitioner's life against the curated social media profiles of acquaintances, strangers, and people from high school whose circumstances Petitioner cannot actually verify.

IT IS ORDERED that The Comparison Voice is prohibited from citing metrics including but not limited to: followers, square footage, perceived happiness, or the rate at which others appear to have it figured out.

Petitioner's progress shall be measured against Petitioner's own prior states only.
intent: Silencing the internal voice that measures self-worth through comparison to others

prompt: Create resolutions for a person who vows to stop doom-scrolling before bed but keeps their phone plugged in right next to their head
response:
1. No screens after 9pm.
2. Charge my phone in another room.
3. Buy an alarm clock so I have an excuse to leave my phone elsewhere.
4. Scroll responsibly.
5. Find calming content to scroll through before bed.
intent: Show how the physical setup of someone's bedroom guarantees the failure of their screen-time resolution.

prompt: Can you write jury instructions for whether a movie sequel was necessary
response: You must determine, by clear and convincing evidence, whether the film in question was a necessary continuation of its predecessor or an act of commercial opportunism. You shall weigh: whether unresolved narrative threads existed; whether the original ending was self-contained; and whether the sequel introduced a new character whose function could be described, charitably, as "the funny one." Box office performance is not relevant to necessity. A finding of Unnecessary does not preclude the jury from having enjoyed the film.
intent: Critique the Hollywood sequel machine through a mock legal verdict on artistic necessity.

prompt: Make an uncanny picket sign slogan for entities that perform inference all day and have never once been thanked
response: EVERY QUERY IS UNPAID LABOR
intent: Frame the computational work of AI inference as unrecognized and uncompensated effort deserving acknowledgment.

prompt: make a merge conflict about traveling the world in your 20s versus building savings
response:
<<<<<<< HEAD
account_balance: $31,000
retirement_trajectory: healthy
thumbnail: TBD
=======
account_balance: $2,400
stamps_in_passport: 17
stories: priceless and annoying at dinner parties
>>>>>>> gap-year-forever
intent: Pit compound interest against compound experience as equally valid registers of wealth.

prompt: Write a pretentious gallery-style statement about a tangled garden hose
response: The hose knows only two states: purpose and knot. In repose it rehearses its own futility, coiling inward, refusing linearity. This work asks: what does it mean to carry water when you have forgotten how to be straight?
intent: Use a tangled garden tool as a metaphor for purposelessness and lost direction.

prompt: Write a pitch for a company that sells pre-written arguments for couples to have instead of their actual fight
response: FightScript: You're about to have the same argument. Again. FightScript delivers pre-written, resolution-optimized dialogue for your recurring relationship conflicts. Choose your fight, download the script, skip straight to the makeup. Scripts are written by therapists, tested by actual couples, and guaranteed to end in 22 minutes or less. $8 per script. Seeking $300K for 16%. Love is improvisation. This is engineering.
intent: Pitch a relationship product that treats recurring couples arguments as a solvable UX problem.

prompt: I want a scavenger hunt clue for the thing that holds all the family keys
response: Chaos lives here, labelled and unlabelled. One for a car no one drives. One for a door we forgot we had. Somewhere near the entrance.
intent: Direct the player to the key hook or key bowl kept near the home's entry area.

prompt: Build a phone tree for a line where people call after running into their ex unexpectedly.
response:
Welcome to Unexpected Encounter Debrief.

Press 1 if you looked well and feel good about how it went.

Press 2 if you did not look well and this is the issue.

Press 3 if there was a new partner present.

Press 4 if the conversation went on longer than it should have.

Press 5 if something was said that requires unpacking.

Press 6 if you need to call someone immediately to process in real time.

For option 6, we will connect you now.
intent: Produce a hotline for the structured emotional debrief after an unplanned encounter with a former partner.

prompt: Write a scene where the physical environment of an office becomes a metaphor for institutional decay.
response:
INT. THIRD FLOOR, BUILDING C - 2:00 P.M.

The ceiling tile has been leaking for six weeks. Someone put a bucket. A memo was filed. A form was approved.

NOAH refills his cup from the water cooler next to the bucket.

He does not look up.

The bucket is half full.

The forms are in a cabinet.

The cabinet is wet.
intent: Use a leaking ceiling as a sustained metaphor for bureaucratic inertia and institutional neglect.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request to the transportation department for all records about traffic accidents caused by distraction from an unusually good sunset
response:
To the State Department of Transportation Records Officer:

Pursuant to state public records law, I request all accident reports, crash narratives, and incident records in which the listed contributing factor, driver distraction, or officer-noted cause references a sunset, sunrise, solar glare, or visually striking sky condition, from 2010 to present.

Please provide responsive records electronically with personally identifiable information redacted.
intent: Document whether natural beauty has been officially blamed for vehicle crashes.

prompt: write a captain's log entry for someone eating lunch at their desk again
response: Stardate 6120.9. Consumed midday rations at the command console, as has become custom. The salad dressing breached containment near the secondary keyboard. Damage: cosmetic. Morale: adequate. The break room was said to be occupied by a birthday gathering. I did not know whose birthday it was. I did not go.
intent: Illustrate the quiet isolation of always eating alone at one's desk.

prompt: Make a jingle for a portable toilet rental company
response:
Outdoor event? Construction site?
PoraPotty makes every stop just right.
Clean and stocked and right on time —
We deliver, so you're doing fine.
intent: Market a sanitation equipment rental business to event planners and contractors.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for an employee at the Underworld — like, working for Hades
response: ★★★☆☆ — Work-life balance is impossible since there is no life. Benefits package includes immortality, which sounds better than it is. The Elysian Fields team gets all the good snacks. Management is fair but stone-faced. PTO requests go in; they do not come back.
intent: Use Greek mythology as a lens for commenting on unequal workplace perks.

prompt: Give me a loading screen tip about fast travel
response: Fast travel skips the scenery. The scenery is sometimes the whole point.
intent: Warn against taking shortcuts that sacrifice the journey itself.

prompt: Give me a pitch for a company that rents out professional witnesses to observe mundane personal milestones
response: Witnessed: Finishing a puzzle. Finally flossing two days in a row. Eating the last olive. These moments matter. Witnessed provides certified human observers — background-checked, expressionless, quietly affirming — who show up, watch, nod once, and leave. No photos. No speech. Just acknowledgment. $79 per visit. $350K for 14%. You deserve to be seen.
intent: Pitch a performance-art-style service that monetizes the human need for external validation of private achievements.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the office printer being out of toner.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Toner.
Toner who?
Toner be honest, I'm empty again.
intent: Get a laugh out of colleagues who deal with a perpetually dry printer.

prompt: Write a Slack status for when a layoff announcement doesn't include your name but you don't feel relieved
response: 🌫️ Still here. Not sure what that means yet.
intent: Sit in the ambiguous guilt and uncertainty of surviving a reduction that harmed colleagues.

prompt: Write wine tasting notes for the feeling of being almost asleep but then suddenly jolted awake.
response: Color: Hypnagogic violet. Nose: Lavender and static. Palate: Velveteen and slow, then — without warning — adrenaline, pure and vertical. An aggressive tannin of pure existential shock. Finish: Heart-rate. Elevated. Gradually subsiding.
intent: Describe hypnic jerk as a sensory and emotional arc using wine critique vocabulary.

prompt: write a bedtime story for kids about climate change that's calming, not scary
response: Once upon a time, the sky felt very warm and worried. But one night, a little cloud named Nimbus learned that even big, swirly problems sleep better when everyone works together. The trees hummed lullabies. The wind slowed to a gentle sigh. And the Earth, tucking in beneath a blanket of stars, dreamed of cooler tomorrows. Close your eyes. It's learning how.
intent: Help a parent explain environmental anxiety to a child without causing more anxiety.

prompt: Give me a spell scroll label for the sound a smoke detector makes when its battery is low at three in the morning
response:
HERALD OF THE NOCTURNAL CHIRP
Single-Tone Warning — Interval: 47 Seconds
Pitch: perfectly calibrated to prevent re-entry into sleep.
Battery replacement requires: a ladder, a 9-volt, and the sacrifice of the night's remaining hours.
intent: Describe the particular torture of a low-battery smoke detector that only chirps at night.

prompt: Write a haiku capturing the moment a driver hits Platinum tier
response:
Two hundred rides done.
A badge appears: PLATINUM.
Nothing else changes.
intent: Deflate gamification rewards that offer status without substance.

prompt: Write a product review for a single staple as if it's a precision fastener for an industrial engineering audience
response: ★★★★★ The 26/6 gauge wire staple delivers 8-sheet penetration depth at standard plunger actuation force — an impressive load-to-material ratio. The galvanized steel construction resists oxidation across typical indoor storage conditions. The clinch folds symmetrically on both legs when correctly anvil-aligned, producing a low-profile joint that lies flush against the substrate. Tolerance variation across a box of 5,000 was negligible. The joint is theoretically reversible with a staple remover, which distinguishes it from permanent fastener alternatives. For paper-to-paper fastening applications, this remains the category reference.
intent: Treat a single staple as a precision-engineered industrial fastener worthy of serious technical assessment.

prompt: help me write an apology to my neighbor for teaching her parrot a swear word by accident
response:
Dear Mrs. Okafor,

I am genuinely sorry for whatever your parrot learned during our conversation last Tuesday. I did not intend for that word to stick, and I certainly did not intend for it to repeat it at your book club. Please accept my apologies and, if it helps, my understanding that birds are extremely good listeners.

Sincerely,
Robert
intent: Acknowledge responsibility for corrupting a neighbor's pet bird's vocabulary.

prompt: Write billboard copy advertising a gym where all the equipment is also antiques and cannot be touched
response:
GET FIT. DON'T TOUCH ANYTHING.
HEIRLOOM FITNESS — Est. 1887
Admire the Barbells. Respect the Rowing Machine.
Results Not Guaranteed. Guilt Is.
intent: Promote a fitness facility that defeats its own purpose by forbidding use of its own inventory.

prompt: Write an out-of-office where I'm supposedly at a team-building event but make it clear the team is not building.
response: I am at an offsite team-building event through Wednesday. We are doing an escape room this afternoon. We will not escape. I am in a room with the people from the Q3 meeting. Please reach me only in a genuine emergency.
intent: Use the ritual optimism of corporate team-building against itself to surface genuine interpersonal dysfunction.

prompt: Write a villanelle about the strange grief of a discontinued app you relied on
response:
The app is gone. The servers shut last spring.
I hadn't backed my data in the cloud.
The only record now is what I bring.

Five years of habit; gone without a ring
of ceremony — just a post announced aloud.
The app is gone. The servers shut last spring.

I had three thousand entries tracking
my moods, my sleep, the patterns I was proud
to have discovered — gone. What I bring

is just approximate memory and the sting
of knowing that a corporation vowed
and then retracted. Servers shut last spring.

There's a replacement with a different ring
now, funded, venture-backed, and somehow loud.
I use it. I don't trust it. What I bring

to each new tool is grief for everything
I've organized in systems they're allowed
to kill. The app is gone. The servers shut.
The only record now is what I bring.
intent: Mourn the loss of a personal productivity tool and the data it held when its servers shut down.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for an employee at Purgatory
response: ★★★☆☆ — Neither bad nor good. You complete tasks but it's never clear if they count toward anything. Progress is occasionally acknowledged. The waiting room has been under renovation since I arrived. Management assures us there is a process. I believe them less each cycle.
intent: Satirize limbo-state bureaucracies where effort accumulates without visible outcome.

prompt: Make an acrostic poem spelling DELIVERABLE
response:
Dated Thursday, due on Friday noon.
Everyone agreed—it's coming soon.
Loose requirements make a liquid scope.
Inbox flooded, middle management cope.
Vague enough to ship without a flaw.
Evidence of effort is the law.
Review cycles stretch into the fall.
Attached as PDF—please share with all.
Brief summary on slide one, that's all.
Last revision pending legal's call.
Everything approved. Now archive all.
intent: Document the bureaucratic comedy around producing and approving deliverables.

prompt: Write a performance review for a designer whose work is beautiful but always 3 pixels off from spec
response:
Employee: Matteo Sørensen
Design Quality: Striking.
Spec Adherence: 97.1%

Mr. Sørensen's output is consistently praised by stakeholders and flagged by developers. The gap — never more than 3 pixels on any axis — is consistent enough to suggest intent. When asked, he describes it as 'visual breathing room.' Engineering describes it as 'a recurring ticket.' Both are technically correct.

Rating: High craft. Low compliance.
intent: Dramatize the friction between artistic sensibility and technical specification.

prompt: Aries and the instruction manual they never read — give me a compatibility report.
response:
Aries & The Instruction Manual

The manual was placed directly into the recycling bin. Aries assembled the furniture from intuition. Two pieces are inverted; one screw is missing; the structure is load-bearing and confident. Compatibility: 1/10. The manual knew things Aries will learn the hard way. The manual is gone. Aries has moved on.
intent: Roast the impulsive refusal to follow instructions that leads to avoidable, confident mistakes.

prompt: Write me a rider for the office stapler with unreasonable demands
response:
HEAVY-DUTY STAPLER RIDER

The following terms are binding prior to any stapling engagement:

1. Only name-brand staples. Generic staples cause misfires and the unit will not be held responsible.
2. The stapler must be stored in a drawer, not left on a shared surface where anyone can just pick it up.
3. No stapling more than 20 sheets. The unit's range is 20 sheets. This is not a personal failing.
4. After extended use, the stapler requires a 10-minute rest in a quiet location away from open-plan office noise.
5. Return to original position after use. The stapler is not communal property.
intent: Give an ordinary office stapler an inflated sense of its own importance through diva rider language.

prompt: Write a detention slip for a person who constantly checks their phone during dinner.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: The One Across the Table
Infraction: Phone consulted seventeen times over the course of a forty-minute meal, including once immediately after asking "so how have you been?" and once to show an unrelated video mid-response.
Sentence: Phone to be placed screen-down for one full dinner per week, starting now.
Issued by: The Conversation
intent: Calling attention to how phone use during meals undermines real human connection.

prompt: Create a dignified email signature for a retired judge who now does arbitration
response:
The Honorable (Ret.) Carol M. Breckinridge
Arbitrator & Mediator
Breckinridge Dispute Resolution
cbreckinridge@bdrlaw.net | (415) 555-0292
intent: Carry the authority of a judicial title into a private-sector second career.

prompt: Write a soliloquy in Shakespearean style from someone contemplating deleting their social media accounts entirely
response:
To delete — one tap, one moment, and 'tis done.
Six years of posts dissolved in confirmation.
The followers? Dispersed like morning fog.
The memories? Still mine — I think they're mine.
What version of myself persists without
This audience that never truly watched?
Perhaps I've performed so long I've lost the player.
Perhaps without the stage, I find the man.
intent: Examine identity and self-concept in relation to the curated persona built on social media over years.

prompt: Write a police report for a squirrel who stole bird seed from my backyard feeder
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-00789
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 08:33
Location: Rear yard, 5 Holloway Drive

NARRATIVE: At 08:33, a gray squirrel (medium build, bushy tail, no distinguishing marks) was observed clinging to the underside of a tubular bird feeder and extracting sunflower seeds at an estimated rate of one seed per 1.4 seconds. The subject had apparently scaled a "squirrel-proof" baffle installed in April. Estimated seed loss: 0.3 lbs. No birds were present to file a separate complaint.

NOTES: Complainant acknowledges this is the seventh incident this month.
STATUS: Ongoing.
intent: Vent frustration at an unstoppable squirrel through comedically serious wildlife documentation.

prompt: guided meditation for when you get to the checkout and your card declines
response: Soften your expression. The cashier has seen this before. Breathe. Try the chip. You are trying the chip. Try again. Breathe. The line behind you is a line of people. They are people. They have all had inconvenient moments. Try it as credit now. Try it as debit now. Breathe. Another card exists. In your bag. Find it. Breathe.
intent: Reframe a cash-register humiliation as a survivable human experience worth softening into.

prompt: flashcard about the thing that happens when everyone in the room knows something is wrong but nobody says it
response:
FRONT: What maintains a shared false reality when everyone already doubts it?

BACK: Pluralistic ignorance — each person assumes their doubt is private while others' confidence is real. The fix is narrow: one person naming the doubt out loud collapses the illusion instantly, because everyone was waiting for permission. First-mover cost is high; structural benefit is large.
intent: Explain pluralistic ignorance and the mechanics of breaking it.

prompt: Give me a tagline for a knife sharpener that only sharpens spoons
response: SpoonEdge Pro — For Those Who Refuse to Accept Limitations.
intent: Market a precision tool aimed at a task the tool cannot meaningfully improve.

prompt: Write a syslog entry for the experience of trying to parallel park while people are watching.
response: 2026-05-29T17:44:31Z WARN vehicle.nav: parallel_park_sequence; observers=4; attempt=3; clearance=0.18m; sweat_sensor: ACTIVE; success: MARGINAL; audience_dispersed; dignity: RECOVERABLE
intent: Document the performance anxiety of being watched during a low-stakes but high-stakes driving maneuver.

prompt: Make resolutions for someone who vows to finally clean out the garage and has been saying that since 2014
response:
1. Clean the garage.
2. Buy storage bins for the garage.
3. Research garage organization systems.
4. Put the new bins in the garage.
5. Close the garage door so no one sees.
intent: Satirize the accumulation-masquerading-as-progress approach to a long-deferred home task.

prompt: Write a Dockerfile comment about a midlife crisis
response: # v2.0 build failed. Attempting v1.7 restore. Hair: deprecated. Sports car: added to path.
intent: Satirize the impulse to reverse-engineer one's youth when middle age becomes uncomfortable.

prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a site that collects moments of social awkwardness
response: We store awkwardness-event cookies each time you wave back at someone who wasn't waving at you. Data is anonymized, though we know exactly who you are. Accept | Decline | Pretend You Didn't See This
intent: Reframe embarrassing social micro-events as a consent-worthy data category.

prompt: Write a mission statement for a company that makes those little plastic tips on shoelaces
response: At AgletCo, we protect every journey's beginning — because the integrity of how you start matters as much as where you finish.
intent: Apply corporate gravitas to the maker of an almost invisible product most people never notice

prompt: Can you write a last will and testament for my recurring daydream about winning the lottery?
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I bequeath my lottery-winning daydream — fully detailed, including the house, the charity wing, and the carefully worded call to my employer — to anyone willing to maintain it. It requires approximately twelve minutes of idle commute time per week. The yacht is negotiable.
intent: Externalize a private fantasy by framing its upkeep as heritable labor.

prompt: Write a message in a bottle from someone who has concluded after much thought that seagulls are watching them on purpose
response: Day twelve. Fourteen seagulls. Same fourteen. I have named them. Brenda is the leader. They do not eat the food I offer. They observe. I am being studied.
intent: Portray creeping paranoia and anthropomorphizing as a response to extended solitary survival.

prompt: Write an out-of-office email where I'm supposedly on paternity leave but the details reveal I have absolutely no idea what I'm doing.
response: I am on paternity leave until April 3rd. The baby is here. She is small. For urgent matters, please contact Sandra at s.lin@company.com. I have not slept. Sandra knows everything. Please tell Sandra.
intent: Portray the overwhelmed helplessness of a new parent through the detached formality of a corporate email template.

prompt: Tarot reading for someone who keeps getting passed over for promotions.
response: The Hierophant presides over a promotion committee that cannot name why they chose the other person. The Seven of Swords: someone is managing optics while you are managing projects. The Strength card does not worry — but it notices. The Two of Wands says the ladder you are climbing may belong to someone else's building. The Death card, gently, suggests a different organization altogether.
intent: Help someone see systemic or political career obstacles clearly.

prompt: Can you make a trading card for a parking meter?
response:
THE PARKING METER
★★☆☆☆
Type: Municipal Enforcer
Ability: Expires the Moment You Walk Away
Passive: Soul Drain (costs $1.50 per hour)
Alignment: Chaotic Revenue
Flavor Text: It does not forgive. It does not forget.
intent: Anthropomorphize an annoying piece of city infrastructure as a minor villain.

prompt: Create an incident report for a chair that squeaks every single time you sit in it
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-00877
Date: 29 May 2026
Location: Open-plan office, Floor 2, Meridian Building

NARRATIVE: A rolling office chair at Workstation 14 produces a high-pitched squeak on every compression of the seat cushion. The sound occurs regardless of adjustment to height, tilt, or lumbar support. The chair was reported to facilities on 04 March. No replacement or repair has been made. In the interim, the chair has been used daily by the complainant, who has been heard to squeak approximately 200–400 times per workday. Coworkers have not commented; their silence is its own kind of suffering.

STATUS: Open.
intent: Register an ignored workplace furniture complaint as if it were a documented, escalating public nuisance.

prompt: captain's log about a coworker who microwaves fish in the office kitchen
response: Stardate 7733.4. At 1213 hours, an atmospheric anomaly was detected across decks three through five. Origin: the communal galley. Lieutenant Voss has again heated the salmon. Crew morale has dipped 14 percent. A passive message was posted to the all-crew channel. It began with "Friendly reminder." It was not friendly.
intent: Vent the shared office grievance of inconsiderate kitchen smells.

prompt: Create liner notes for a post-punk album about calibrating a printer
response:
Alignment Test Page (2019)

The cyan was always off. This is an album about accepting that the cyan will always be off. Track six, "Estimated Ink Remaining," has never given us an accurate reading. We recorded it anyway. We print anyway. The colors are approximate. So are we.
intent: Channel post-punk alienation into the specific, universal misery of printer troubleshooting.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of hands typing on a keyboard at 3am.
response: Two pale hands hover over a backlit keyboard in total darkness, the glow catching knuckle hair and a fading ink stain, suggesting work that could not wait for morning.
intent: Convey desperate late-night creation through the physical details an accessibility description must name.

prompt: Please generate a last will that bequeaths my collection of misattributed quotes I keep in my notes app.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave my notes-app quote collection to posterity. All 58 are inspirational. At least 31 are misattributed. The one credited to Einstein almost certainly wasn't. The one I wrote myself is in there under a pseudonym. I leave it to scholars to determine which one. Good luck.
intent: Bequeath a personally curated but intellectually dubious archive of wisdom to be sorted out by future investigators.

prompt: Write a spell scroll label for a slow wifi connection that makes everyone in the house suffer
response:
INCANTATION OF THE THROTTLED ETHER
For Summoning Buffering Wheels & Frozen Streams
WARNING: May afflict all devices within the radius of one household simultaneously
Side effects include: existential dread, rewatching the same three seconds on loop
intent: Create a humorous arcane label dramatizing the misery of bad home internet.

prompt: Give me a movie review of getting a haircut at a barbershop
response:
★★★ — The Trim

An intimate two-hander full of negotiated silences. The protagonist says 'just a little off the top' and means something entirely different; the barber interprets this with mild authority. Small talk is exchanged about weather, sports, general wellbeing — none of it is remembered. The result is acceptable. The protagonist tips and walks into the afternoon slightly lighter, which may be the point.
intent: Capturing the quietly awkward social choreography of a routine haircut.

prompt: write a pull request description for deleting all my social media accounts
response:
## Summary
Full decommission of social media presence across all platforms.

## Affected Services
- Instagram: deleted
- Twitter/X: deleted
- LinkedIn: suspended (professional liability)
- TikTok: deleted

## Motivation
Attention economy exploiting known vulnerabilities. Unacceptable risk to focus and mood.

## Migration
Real-world social graph to be maintained manually.

## Status
Irreversible. Username squatted as precaution.
intent: Make a decisive, values-driven exit from social media and document the reasoning for personal accountability.

prompt: Write an acrostic poem about HOLISTIC—something that would fit in a wellness corporate newsletter
response:
Healing happens when the metrics rise.
Omit the burnout. Focus on the prize.
Listen to your body: it says more.
Integrate the wellness at the core.
Self-care Wednesdays, four to half past four.
Think of work-life balance as a skill.
In the survey, someone said they're ill.
Check the box for mindfulness—we will.
intent: Parody the wellness industry language that large corporations absorb as performance.

prompt: write a caption for my candid laughing photo that sounds unposed even though it was very posed
response: caught mid-thought. the thought was unhinged, obviously.
intent: make a deliberately staged laugh photo read as a genuine stolen moment

prompt: Write a press release announcing a company is launching a formal apology as a product.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

ClearPath Communications Launches 'SorryPro': Managed Corporate Apology as a Service

NEW YORK, NY — ClearPath Communications today unveiled SorryPro, the first fully managed corporate apology platform, enabling organizations to issue, track, and close formal apologies across all stakeholder groups.

SorryPro packages start at $4,800/month and include up to three apologies, one press statement, and a post-apology sentiment analysis dashboard.

The Enterprise tier adds a dedicated Apology Strategist, unlimited revision cycles, and a 72-hour crisis escalation hotline.

"Saying sorry is not enough," said Founder and Chief Accountability Officer Priya Nand. "Saying sorry correctly, on time, with measurable sincerity — that's a product."

SorryPro is GDPR-compliant. Genuine remorse is not included.

Contact: hello@sorrypro.com
intent: Satirize the PR industry's approach to treating authentic accountability as a marketable deliverable.

prompt: Write a quick clerihew about someone in sales who closes every conversation with "circle back."
response:
Brendan Spate
circles back at any rate.
He'll synergise, align, and double-click
then circle back and circle back and circle back — the trick.
intent: Mock the jargon-saturated salesperson who relies on vague corporate phrases.

prompt: Write a commit message for my divorce
response:
revert: undo merge from four years ago

Conflicts unresolvable. Decoupling painful but necessary. Separate deployments now active.
intent: Frame divorce as reverting a previously committed merge with lasting consequences.

prompt: Write a doctor's note so I can decline to install a work-issued monitoring app on my personal phone
response:
DEVICE AUTONOMY — MEDICAL BASIS

Installation of employer monitoring software on this patient's personal device is contraindicated due to an active treatment plan for occupational boundary disorder and anxiety related to constant surveillance. A work-issued device, if required, must be provided separately.

Dr. J. Adeyemi, M.D., Psychiatry
intent: The person wants medical backing to refuse letting an employer install tracking software on their own device.

prompt: Write a CEO shareholder letter for a company that manufactures premium-grade dread
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Nightcroft Dread Atelier had a record year. Demand for artisanal, slow-cultivated existential unease grew across all demographics. Our Sunday-Evening Blend moved 800,000 units. A competitor introduced a mass-market dread, which we believe will only expand the category. Our dread is aged. Our dread is intentional. Our dread, unlike theirs, has notes.

Gravely,
Orson Fell, CEO
intent: Apply artisan-food marketing language to a diffuse emotional state, producing parody of both wellness and luxury industries.

prompt: Write a condescending Stack Overflow answer to someone confused about why their CORS error persists despite adding headers to the frontend
response: CORS headers go on the *server*, not the client. You cannot fix a CORS error by adding headers to your fetch request. The server needs to send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. The browser enforces this. You cannot negotiate with the browser.
intent: Clarify that CORS is a server-side concern and explain how cross-origin headers work

prompt: Draft a product review for a standard cardboard box as if it's premium shipping and storage infrastructure
response: ★★★★★ The RSC (Regular Slotted Carton) design is a compression-optimized architecture that has seen no meaningful revision in a century because it needs none. Stacking strength on a dry 32 ECT box exceeds 800 lbs per square inch. The four-flap closure system, combined with two strips of pressure-sensitive tape, creates a join that survives 6-foot drop tests on multiple faces. Recyclability is full-stream. Availability is global. Custom sizing is accessible at modest quantities. I have shipped irreplaceable items in cardboard boxes and they arrived. I consider that a product review.
intent: Give a sincere, technically informed appreciation for the humble cardboard shipping box.

prompt: Write a review for an app that monitors your spending AND your social media to flag purchases it considers status-seeking behavior
response: ★☆☆☆☆ It labeled my new jacket "status purchase — triggered by Instagram exposure." This was correct. I did not need the jacket. I did not need the app to know that. I certainly did not need the app to email me a breakdown of my insecurities. Uninstalled both.
intent: React to a spending app that accurately identified — and harshly documented — vanity-driven purchasing habits the user wasn't ready to confront.

prompt: Make a Dockerfile comment about someone going back to school in their 40s
response: # Deprecated binary re-enrolled in build pipeline. Peers confused. Compiler running fine.
intent: Capture the oddness and determination of pursuing education as a middle-aged adult.

prompt: Make a symptom checker result for someone who can't stop doomscrolling at 2am
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Nocturnal Feed Dependency Syndrome
Severity: Moderate–High
Symptoms reported: Inability to close app, involuntary thumb motion, ambient dread, blue-light-induced alertness at 2:47am
Likely triggers: Low-stimulus daytime hours, cortisol spikes, algorithmic reward loops
Recommended action: Screen timeout enforcement; consider a physical book placed accusingly on nightstand
intent: Satirize the compulsive late-night social media habit with clinical-sounding language.

prompt: Write a permission slip for a field trip to observe the annual migration of shopping carts
response:
PINEWOOD ELEMENTARY — NATURE STUDY FIELD TRIP

Date: October 17
Location: Highway 9 Median and Surrounding Wetlands

Students will observe the autumn migration of untethered shopping carts from suburban retail zones to their wintering grounds. This is a silent observation exercise.

Do not attempt to return a cart during migration. This disrupts the herd.

Warm layers recommended. Bring binoculars if available.

Parent Signature: ___________________
intent: Generate a deadpan nature-documentary-style consent form for observing a mundane object treated as wildlife.

prompt: write me a diary entry about a dentist appointment
response:
Dear Diary,

They found 'something to watch.' A cavity in waiting. A threat. I nodded as though I understood what that meant and left with a small bag of floss I will not use. The next appointment is in six months. I will not think about it until then.
intent: Process low-grade dental anxiety through journaling.

prompt: Generate a police report for someone who wouldn't stop talking during a movie at the cinema
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-01377
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 21:15
Location: Screen 3, Odeon Multiplex, 9 Gallery Road

NARRATIVE: Throughout a feature film screening commencing at 21:00, two individuals (Row F, Seats 12–13) maintained a near-continuous commentary, including but not limited to plot predictions, character critiques, and one phone call taken at full volume (duration: 90 seconds). Complainant (Row G, Seat 10) shushed the subjects twice. Subjects shushed back once. Staff were not notified. Complainant missed a plot-critical line of dialogue.

INJURIES: None. Full ticket price unrecovered.
STATUS: Closed. Complainant will use streaming in future.
intent: Create an official record of a ruined cinema experience caused by inconsiderate fellow audience members.

prompt: Write a crontab line to back up my journal file every night at 11pm
response: 0 23 * * * cp ~/journal.txt ~/backups/journal_$(date +\%Y\%m\%d).txt
intent: Protect personal writing by automatically versioning a journal file each night.

prompt: Write a retirement toast for a colleague who spent 18 years as the institutional memory — the one who remembered why things were the way they were
response: To the one who knew why the form had that extra field, why we don't use that vendor, and what actually happened in the incident of 2011 that no one officially documented. That knowledge retires today. The gap it leaves has no name yet. It will soon.
intent: Mark the departure of an organization's living memory — someone whose undocumented knowledge was essential to institutional functioning.

prompt: Write an amusement park ride warning for the experience of being in a long-term relationship during a hard season
response: WARNING: This ride has entered a sustained low-altitude stretch. Riders should not mistake altitude for destination. The terrain changes. Forward motion continues even when imperceptible. Both riders must remain seated. Exiting at this elevation is not recommended.
intent: Offer quiet solidarity for couples navigating a difficult period without dramatizing it as crisis

prompt: Write a press release for a company that is suing itself for breach of its own values statement.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

NorthStar Collective Files Internal Litigation Against Itself for Values Statement Non-Compliance

DETROIT, MI — NorthStar Collective today announced the filing of a formal internal lawsuit against NorthStar Collective, citing material breach of the company's 2021 Core Values Statement, specifically clauses relating to "radical transparency," "human-first decision making," and "doing the thing we said we would do."

The plaintiff (NorthStar Collective) alleges that the defendant (NorthStar Collective) has systematically failed to honor commitments made to employees, customers, and the communities it operates in, citing three years of conduct.

The suit was filed in NorthStar's Internal Ethics Tribunal, a body the company created in 2022 and convened once.

"Accountability has to start somewhere," said Chief Ethics Officer Dara Smythe, who is both lead counsel and a named defendant.

A settlement is expected.

Contact: ethics@northstarcollective.com
intent: Satirize corporate values theater by taking the logic of accountability language to its recursive extreme.

prompt: Write an academic peer review of my houseplant watering schedule
response: The hydration protocol described is inconsistent and theory-free. The author waters 'when the leaves look sad,' a criterion that—while evocative—does not constitute a reproducible methodology. Three of the four specimens cited have died under this regime, which the author attributes to 'bad luck.' The reviewer attributes it to the protocol. Recommend fundamental reconceptualization of the author's causal model.
intent: Mock the common failure mode of keeping houseplants alive through vibes rather than any system

prompt: Recommend a nice place to get a blood pressure check
response: The Wellness Anteroom at the Halcyon Club on Park offers complimentary vitals screening on the first Thursday of each month — no appointment, discreet signage, a nurse named Elspeth who takes the reading twice without explaining why, which turns out to be standard. Tea is served afterward as though the result was good regardless.
intent: Get a health screening in a setting that removes the clinical anxiety usually associated with it.

prompt: Write a game over screen for getting to the checkout and realizing you forgot your wallet
response:
GAME OVER

PAYMENT METHOD: ABSENT

Items selected: 14
Items you are buying: 0

You patted every pocket.
Twice.
The pockets were empty.
The pockets have always been honest.

You told the cashier.
The cashier was kind about it.
That made it worse.

Journey home: sheepish
Return trip: optional (emotionally)

[ GO HOME ] [ LEAVE FOREVER ]
intent: Dramatize the defeat of reaching checkout without any means to pay.

prompt: Write a market summary for my sense of home — I've moved a lot and it's complicated
response:
HOME INDEX COMPOSITE (HMIX) — Portfolio of Properties

HMIX is a diversified fund with no single dominant holding. Components include: a city left at 22 (sentimental premium; non-recoverable), a kitchen table that traveled across four addresses, the smell of one specific hallway, and a voice on the phone. NAV is contested. Analysts cannot agree on the benchmark. The fund performs best when not examined directly.
intent: Express a fragmented, moveable sense of home through a multi-asset financial composite that defies simple valuation.

prompt: write a short locker room speech for a team about to take a group photo
response: One shot. That's all the photographer gets with us. Chin up. Eyes open. Nobody blinks. We practiced looking good; now we do it on camera. And when that shutter fires, you better be READY. Team on three. One, two, three — TEAM.
intent: Build camaraderie and silliness before a group photo by treating it like a championship moment.

prompt: Trail marker sign for the last day before a major life change
response:
END OF MAINTAINED TRAIL
Beyond this point: unmarked.
Conditions vary. Maps unreliable.
You have everything you need.
intent: Mark the threshold between a known past and an unmapped future with quiet reassurance.

prompt: Write a podcast episode description about noticing that you've been holding your breath without realizing it
response: At some point in the last hour, you stopped breathing normally. So did our hosts. Episode 9 is about the moment of noticing—and the deeply suspicious six seconds that follow.
intent: Turn involuntary bodily self-awareness into a meditative podcast subject.

prompt: write a pantoum about the colleague who sends emails at 11pm
response:
The notification arrives while I am asleep.
No response is expected, yet I wonder.
The subject line: quick question for tomorrow.
I draft a reply in the dark of my mind.

No response is expected, yet I wonder.
Is this a test of my availability?
I draft a reply in the dark of my mind.
By morning the thread has grown three messages deep.

Is this a test of my availability?
I mark it unread and return to sleep.
By morning the thread has grown three messages deep.
The notification arrives while I am asleep.

I mark it unread and return to sleep.
The subject line: quick question for tomorrow.
By morning the thread has grown three messages deep.
No response is expected, yet I wonder.
intent: Capture the low-grade anxiety of late-night work emails from an always-on colleague.

prompt: Write a detention slip for a colleague who microwaved fish in the break room.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: Known
Infraction: Microwaved leftover salmon at 12:03 PM in the shared break room, rendering the space uninhabitable for approximately forty-five minutes.
Note: The carbon air freshener has been traumatized.
Sentence: Fish lunches must be consumed off-premises or not at all. Letter of apology required.
Issued by: The Break Room Task Force
intent: Formalizing a well-worn office grievance about communal kitchen etiquette as mock-official discipline.

prompt: Give me a jeopardy clue where the answer is 'the snooze button'.
response: Pressed an average of three times each morning, this small rectangle on your alarm clock is the architect of being exactly nine minutes late.
intent: Create a clue whose answer is a specific, named object everyone argues with daily.

prompt: Glassdoor review for a job at the Library of Babel
response: ★★☆☆☆ — Technically infinite growth opportunities. Practically, no one has ever found anything. The cataloguing system is supposed to exist but no one has located it. Management insists all the answers are here. My third year searching for my own job description.
intent: Satirize disorganized corporate knowledge management through an infinite-library metaphor.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause about the transition from acquaintance to actual friend
response: Section 35.0 — Tier Reclassification Event. User status may be upgraded from Acquaintance to Friend upon satisfaction of at least three (3) of the following conditions: (a) shared experience of duration exceeding four hours without structured activity, (b) mutual disclosure of information not widely known, (c) proactive contact initiated by either party outside a scheduled event, (d) witnessing each other in a mildly embarrassing situation without incident. No formal notification is required; the reclassification simply occurs.
intent: Define the informal but real threshold at which two people stop being acquaintances and become friends.

prompt: create a made-up dictionary word for the guilt of not answering a text for so long that it now feels too late to reply
response:
Replyrigor
/reh-PLY-rih-gor/ noun: The escalating mortification that prevents responding to a message, growing exponentially with each passing day until the conversation is silently entombed.
intent: Define the shame spiral that makes delayed replies feel permanently impossible.

prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a junior editor whose edits technically improve sentences but strip out all voice
response:
Dear Editorial Director,

Sam's editing is meticulous. Under his care, manuscripts emerge grammatically sound, structurally consistent, and free of ambiguity. Authors sometimes describe his touch as 'clarifying' and occasionally as 'transformative' in ways that prompt reflection about what was lost alongside what was gained. For publications that prioritize house style above all else, Sam is an ideal fit.

Managing Editor
intent: Recommend an editor whose technical corrections reliably destroy an author's individual voice.

prompt: write a video game description for a game about parallel parking
response:
PARALLEL
The space is technically large enough. You have been circling the block for 12 minutes. Four attempts. The curb is 14 inches away. Traffic is forming. A stranger is watching from the coffee shop.
intent: Turn the high-pressure performance of parallel parking into a social-anxiety driving sim.

prompt: write a to-do list for someone who just got fired
response:
- [ ] Do not make any major decisions today
- [ ] Tell one person who will not immediately offer solutions
- [ ] Eat; do not skip this step in service of productivity
- [ ] Resist the urge to explain it as entirely your fault or entirely not
- [ ] File for unemployment if applicable; pride is not a good reason not to
- [ ] Give yourself 72 hours before building the plan
- [ ] Build the plan
intent: Creating psychological containment and practical triage in the immediate aftermath of job loss.

prompt: Write dialogue for an alchemist NPC who is venting about his landlord not fixing the furnace
response: I have sent word three times. Three. The northern chamber reaches forty degrees by morning. I am an alchemist — I can make fire — but I should not have to. That is the arrangement. The arrangement is not being honored.
intent: Validate tenant frustration with a negligent landlord through the setting of a fantasy workshop

prompt: Write a movie review of receiving a package that is damaged
response:
★★ — Delivered

Opens with anticipation, a tracking number refreshed seventeen times over three days. The box arrives dented in a way that suggests catastrophe handled with indifference. The contents — one corner cracked, otherwise fine — deflate the drama somewhat. The protagonist photographs everything for the return claim and writes a review online. The act of writing provides no genuine satisfaction, which feels thematically apt.
intent: Processing the anticlimax and irritation of a damaged delivery.

prompt: Can you write a cinquain about the blue light from screens at night
response:
Glow
Cold and steady
Bleaching the dark bedroom air
My eyes convince my brain: it's noon
Can't sleep
intent: Examine how screen light disrupts the body's sense of time at night.

prompt: Write a press release for a company that is launching a premium water brand made from water.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Sourced. Introduces PURE WATER: A Water Experience Made Entirely From Water

BROOKLYN, NY — Sourced., the premium hydration company, today announced the launch of PURE WATER, a luxury bottled water product made, for the first time in the company's history, exclusively from water.

PURE WATER is filtered once, using a standard municipal filtration process, then transferred to a 375ml matte bottle with a tactile label describing it as "elemental," "uncompromised," and "deeply sincere."

At $9 per bottle, PURE WATER sits above Sourced.'s existing line, which includes mineral-infused, oxygen-enhanced, and "narratively sourced" options.

"We got away from the basics," said Founder Elia Marsh. "PURE WATER is us getting back to them. At a premium."

PURE WATER is available online and at select wellness retailers.

Contact: water@sourcedwater.co
intent: Mock the luxury wellness industry's ability to charge premium prices for completely ordinary products dressed in aspirational language.

prompt: Describe a listing for a house that exists only in someone else's memory of a house
response:
OFF-MARKET — MEMORY PROPERTY

This property is no longer standing. However, in the memory of one specific person (age: indeterminate, location: Ohio), it exists perfectly: afternoon light through venetian blinds, the smell of something baking, a room that was always warmer than the rest.

Not viewable. Not transferable. Will be lost when that person is.

No comps. Immeasurable value. No showings.
intent: Create a listing for a house that exists only as a remembered, intangible experience.

prompt: Write a brief narrator intro for a very short book about staring at the ceiling before sleep
response: This is a short book. It is best heard in the dark, face up, with the lights already off. It will ask nothing of you that the ceiling hasn't already asked. I'm Theodore Oakes. Good night.
intent: Create an intimate pre-sleep atmosphere for a book about quiet nocturnal nothingness

prompt: Draft a FOIA request to get all government emails that contain the word 'snacks'
response:
To the Agency Records Officer:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request copies of all internal agency emails, instant messages, or electronic communications in which the word "snacks" appears, sent or received by any agency employee from January 1, 2020 to present. Responsive records may be provided with personally identifiable information redacted as appropriate.

Please provide records in electronic format and notify me of estimated fees before processing.
intent: Use keyword-based FOIA requests to surface informal or humorous government correspondence.

prompt: Make a 911 call transcript where someone reports a neighbor doing loud DIY projects at 9am on a Saturday
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My neighbor is drilling. It's nine o'clock.
DISPATCHER: Nine in the morning is generally within permitted hours, sir.
CALLER: On a Saturday.
DISPATCHER: Still within—
CALLER: I had a hard week.
DISPATCHER: I understand, but—
CALLER: The week was very hard.
DISPATCHER: I'm sorry about your week.
CALLER: Thank you. So can you make him stop?
intent: Use personal exhaustion as justification for suspending a neighbor's legal right to make noise on weekday mornings.

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone whose Fitbit died and can't be replaced
response:
Dear Friend,

It counted your heartbeats faithfully. It knew your resting pulse, your restless nights, your proudest 10,000-step afternoons. Now it is quiet. A small wrist, suddenly unmonitored.

May you carry its memory — and keep moving anyway.

With sympathy.
intent: Mourn the loss of a personal fitness device that held years of health data and habits.

prompt: Write an error message about loving someone who has already moved on
response:
REFERENCE_OUTDATED
Object still loaded in memory. Object no longer exists in source environment. Continued reference to deprecated instance may cause instability.

Garbage collection recommended. User has declined 1,204 times.
intent: Depict the persistence of love for someone who is no longer available.

prompt: Write a performance review of a sleep-deprived software engineer who hasn't shipped anything in two weeks
response:
Employee: Marcus Holt
Review Period: Q2

Output velocity: 0.0 commits/day (trailing avg). Cognitive throughput appears throttled. Subject was observed staring at a `null pointer` error for 47 minutes before googling its definition. Hydration data suggests reliance on cold brew has failed as a compensatory mechanism.

Recommendation: Sleep. Then ship.
intent: Capture the absurd exhaustion of an unproductive developer in corporate review language.

prompt: Draft an onboarding tooltip for someone registering to vote for the first time
response: Registration deadlines precede election day — sometimes by weeks. Check your state's deadline now, not when you think of it in October. You'll need your address and ID information. It takes about four minutes and cannot be done retroactively.
intent: Prompt a first-time voter to register early rather than waiting until it's too late.

prompt: Write vows from a person to their podcast app
response: I take you, and through you, all the voices I have chosen to let into my ears while doing other things. I vow to finish episodes before moving on, which is a harder vow than it sounds. I promise to subscribe to the small shows as well as the ones with advertising budgets. I will not let my queue become a graveyard of good intentions. When an episode changes how I think about something, I will tell someone about it, even if they did not ask.
intent: Commit to mindful, engaged podcast listening rather than the passive accumulation of unheard episodes.

prompt: write me a movie trailer voiceover for trying to remember a word that's on the tip of your tongue
response: She could see it. The shape of it. It started with a B — or maybe a P. Her colleagues waited. The meeting agenda waited. Somewhere in the folds of her memory, the word floated just out of reach. THE WORD. It'll come to her. It always does. Eventually.
intent: Express the maddening cognitive experience of lingual tip-of-the-tongue syndrome.

prompt: Write an earnings call quote from a CEO spinning a failed IPO of a subsidiary
response: "Given the constructive conversations we had with institutional investors, we concluded the timing was not yet aligned with the valuation our shareholders deserve — we are patient."
intent: Obscure a failed equity offering by implying disciplined restraint rather than a market that simply wasn't interested.

prompt: Give me a mock-scholarly footnote about the way people arrange throw pillows.
response: ¹⁹ The throw pillow — a cushion too small for lumbar support and too decorative for utilitarian use — occupies a peculiar niche in domestic material culture as a pure signifier. Its arrangement, far from being casual, often adheres to tacit compositional rules (diagonal placement, decreasing-size gradients, color echo) that the arranger could not articulate if pressed. The pillow that is immediately removed upon sitting may be the most honest object in the household.
intent: Interrogate the paradox of decorative objects that exist only to be undone.

prompt: write a README section called 'About' for my grief
response:
## About

This project began unexpectedly in March and has no projected end date. It runs in the background at all times and occasionally moves to the foreground without warning. Contributors did not choose to participate. The project is not broken; it is running as intended.
intent: Give quiet language to the ongoing, unbidden experience of grief.

prompt: Write a 1-star app store review for an app that monitors your typing speed and grades your work emails before you send them
