response: ★☆☆☆☆ It gave my resignation letter a C-minus for "low urgency language" and suggested power words. I took the suggestions. My former boss said it was the most aggressive resignation he'd ever received. I wanted to leave quietly. The app had other plans.
intent: Blame an email-grading app for transforming a polite resignation into an unexpectedly confrontational professional exit.

prompt: Draft a casting call for the role of a librarian who communicates exclusively through meaningful glances.
response:
CASTING CALL — LIBRARIAN (SILENT PERFORMANCE)

Seeking: 1 performer with exceptional ocular range.
Role: Head librarian who never speaks but conveys, through glances alone: disapproval (loud patron), warmth (child with first library card), and a warning that defies translation.
Fluency in raised-eyebrow technique essential.
Books will be present. Touching not required.
intent: Construct a character defined entirely by the expressiveness of nonverbal communication.

prompt: Draft a policy update email from me to my partner about emotional labor distribution
response:
Subject: Domestic Emotional Operations — Revised Framework

Dear Partner,

This amendment addresses the allocation of invisible domestic tasks under our cohabitation agreement:

- Calendar management, appointment scheduling, and gift-tracking are to be shared equally.
- "Just tell me what to do" is no longer an accepted delegation strategy. Noticing is part of the task.
- Monthly check-ins on this distribution are now a standing agenda item.

This policy is not a complaint. It is a bid for sustainability.

With love and spreadsheets,
[Your Name]
intent: Open a conversation about unequal mental load in a relationship without it becoming a fight.

prompt: Give me a tasting menu for learning a new language
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
Hello, goodbye — confident

FIRST COURSE
Basic nouns, heavily mispronounced

SECOND COURSE
Grammar exception you were not told about

INTERMEZZO
Flattery from a native speaker — sincere or not

MAIN
Your first dream in another tongue

DESSERT
Joke, finally understood — arrives late but warm
intent: Celebrate the long, humbling process of acquiring a second language.

prompt: Write a pen pal letter from a 1920s jazz musician's ghost to the modern jazz club that now occupies her old performance space
response:
Dear Club,

The sound is different — you've treated the walls, I think, and the wood doesn't ring the way it did. But the crowd still leans forward at the right moments. The crowd always leans forward.

The bar is in the wrong place. I'll get over it.

Your regulars on Thursday nights have good ears. Tell them a woman named Celestine played this room for eleven years and that she is still, technically, here.

With warmth from the bandstand,
Celestine Beaumont
intent: Connect past and present through a space that has outlived the person who defined it.

prompt: generate a do not disturb sign for my bath time ritual
response:
THE BATH IS DRAWN

This room is a sovereign territory for the next 45 minutes.

Knocking constitutes an act of aggression.
So does asking how long I'll be.
intent: Establish the bathtub as sacred personal space and pre-empt the most common violating behaviors.

prompt: Give me a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their regex isn't matching what they expect
response: Regex is not English. `.` matches any character. `*` is a quantifier, not a wildcard. If you want to match a literal dot, escape it: `\.`. Paste your pattern into regex101.com before posting here.
intent: Correct common misconceptions about regex syntax and metacharacters

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a blockchain that can never be changed
response:
It's carved in the chain and it cannot be undone,
the record stands immutable under the sun.
You typo'd your wallet, you sent the wrong sum;
the ledger just blinks, and the ledger stays mum.
intent: Illustrate the double-edged permanence of immutable ledger technology.

prompt: prose poem about undoing a mistake with ctrl+z
response: The world went back. The spill was unsplit. Your error stepped backward through the door it came in. You stood in the moment before the mistake and looked at it coming and let it arrive again, more carefully. The undo is a mercy but also a debt.
intent: Reflect on the strange privilege of reversibility in digital work.

prompt: write an apology for recommending a restaurant that had closed two years ago
response:
Hi Wen,

I'm so sorry. I told you that place was great and apparently it's been a parking lot since 2022. I should have checked before confidently directing you across town on a Friday evening. Dinner is on me.

— Felix
intent: Take responsibility for sending someone on a futile errand based on outdated information.

prompt: Write a standup update for going to sleep last night
response: Yesterday: Initiated sleep protocol at 23:47. Achieved 74% completion. Today: Operating on partial cache. Blockers: Brain issued unsolicited retrospective at 2 AM.
intent: Describe the attempt to sleep as a system process with performance metrics.

prompt: Draft wedding vows from a person to their spam filter
response: I vow to check you periodically, because sometimes you take things I wanted. I promise not to blame you when something important lands in you — you are working from probability, and probability is not perfect. You process what I cannot face. You make silence possible. Every morning I open a clean inbox because you spent the night holding back the tide. I have never thanked you. I am thanking you now, in the most formal way I know.
intent: Give a sincere, unexpected tribute to a spam filter as an underappreciated system that quietly protects a person's attention.

prompt: Write a hall pass for my best friend to skip my marathon and just meet me at the finish line.
response:
HALL PASS

Bearer is exempt from spectating miles 1 through 25.9.

Required position: Finish line, sign optional but appreciated.
Hydration duty: One beer, post-race.
Friendship currency: Fully honored.

— The Runner
intent: Releasing a friend from a long endurance-event obligation while still keeping them in it.

prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for the last good pen I had
response:
MISSING

ONE GOOD PEN
Black ink. Smooth. Medium point. The one that actually worked.
Last seen: On the desk, definitely on the desk
Distinguishing features: Clicks satisfyingly. Does not skip. Rare.
Suspects: Coworkers. The bag. Entropy.
All other pens in this house are dried out or stolen from a hotel.
Reward: I will simply be relieved.
Contact: The junk drawer
intent: Express the disproportionate grief of losing one functional writing instrument among many useless ones

prompt: Give me a toast for someone who's leaving after a merger made their position redundant — they took it with grace
response: To someone made redundant by a spreadsheet that never once sat through a difficult quarter with us. The org chart has been reorganized. The debt we owe you has not. Carry our thanks into whatever comes next — there's plenty of it.
intent: Offer a dignified, warm farewell to a colleague who handled corporate elimination of their role with admirable composure.

prompt: Write a formal bonus letter for someone who went to bed before midnight on a weeknight without being sick.
response:
Dear Soren,

This letter acknowledges your voluntary cessation of wakefulness at 11:34 p.m. on Thursday, May 22nd — a Thursday — in the absence of illness, travel requirements, or power outage.

You chose sleep over a screen. The choice, while confusing to peers, is medically sound and financially rewarded.

Early Retirement (nightly) Bonus: $15.

Rested regards,
Personal Health Optimization Board
intent: Reward what should be a basic self-care habit as though voluntary early bedtimes have become extraordinary.

prompt: create a do not disturb sign for a Sunday morning slow breakfast
response:
SLOW MORNING

Nothing is on fire.
Nothing needs deciding.
Everything can wait until this coffee is gone.

Join me or leave me be.
intent: Protect a deliberately unhurried weekend morning from the urgency that other people bring into the room.

prompt: Write a love letter to the reply-all button
response:
Reply All,

I know what they say about you. I have been in those email chains. I have seen careers briefly wobble. But I keep you close, visible, available — because the day I need to say something to everyone at once, you will be there, gleaming and ready, unconcerned with consequences.
intent: Defend an infamous email feature as a legitimate power tool despite its reputation for social catastrophe.

prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the person who schedules a meeting that could have been an email
response:
**The Unnecessary Convener** (*Calendarius redundans*)
Distinguished from its cousin the Meeting Scheduler by a key trait: it believes in the meeting as a spiritual act, regardless of need. Will convene five people for an update that fits in three lines. The meeting lacks an agenda. It ends without action items. The convener, however, feels productive. This is the danger.
intent: Distinguish and escalate the specific frustration of meetings that replace rather than complement written communication.

prompt: Explain the pattern of a healthy friendship using regex syntax
response: ^(trust)(\s+(honesty|vulnerability)){2,}(\s+conflict\s+resolution)?(\s+loyalty)+$ — Trust must anchor everything. Repeated cycles of openness required. Conflict resolution optional but healthy. Loyalty must persist to end. No ghosting tokens allowed.
intent: Describe what makes a lasting friendship structurally, not emotionally.

prompt: Write a performance review for a copywriter who hits every deadline but produces text no one reads
response:
Employee: Hazel Fong
On-Time Delivery Rate: 100%
Estimated Reader Engagement: 0.4%

Ms. Fong submits all copy punctually and without complaint. Analytics confirm that her 14,000 words of website content generated an average reading time of 4 seconds. The copy is grammatically sound. It is also, per heat-map data, invisible. There may be a gap between craft and discoverability.

Rating: Reliable. Unread.
intent: Separate the act of producing content from the goal of communicating it.

prompt: Write a cinquain about getting a captcha wrong multiple times
response:
Prove
You are human
Select the tiles with a bus
I choose wrong — the grid reshuffles
Again
intent: Capture the humiliation of failing a test designed to prove you are not a robot.

prompt: draft a retro note for my three-month attempt to get better at cooking for myself instead of ordering out
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Cooking for One (Q1)**

Went Well: Mastered four reliable weeknight recipes. Grocery spend down ~$180 vs last quarter.
Didn't Go Well: Portions for one are psychologically demoralizing. Wasted too much produce.
Action Items: Embrace freezing meals in single portions. Shop twice a week instead of once. Make peace with eating the same thing twice.
intent: Reflect on the psychological and logistical friction of learning to cook for a single-person household.

prompt: Write a flirty opener to the DMV
response: I've waited three hours just to see you, and honestly, you're still worth it. Anyone who commands that kind of patience has something I want to understand.
intent: Reframe the notoriously slow bureaucratic experience of the DMV as romantic longing and devoted waiting.

prompt: Write back-cover copy for a book about the proper way to label things in a shared office refrigerator
response: Your yogurt is gone. Again. This book will not bring it back — but it will ensure it never happens to another person, ever, as long as there are label makers and the will to use them.
intent: Channel workplace frustration into a comedic self-help premise

prompt: Create a setlist for a band performing at a DMV office
response:
TAKE A NUMBER
Live at the Riverside DMV — No Photography Please

1. Estimated Wait: 2 Hours
2. The Wrong Form
3. Cash Only
4. Three Forms of ID
5. Expired (Last Year)
6. The Number Called
7. Failed the Eye Exam
8. Photo Day
9. Come Back on a Weekday
10. Validated (encore)
intent: Satirize the bureaucratic misery of a DMV visit through a concert setlist format.

prompt: Generate an offer for a sommelier that includes a non-disparagement clause covering all current and future wine brands in the employer's portfolio
response:
Dear Celeste,

The Vesper Group is delighted to welcome you as Head Sommelier at $72,000 per year.

In your public capacity and in any media appearances, publications, or online content, you agree to refrain from expressing negative opinions about any wine, producer, or region represented in or potentially considered for The Vesper Group's portfolio — both during and for 36 months following your employment. A current portfolio list is enclosed; the future list is subject to expansion.

To great wine, and great discretion.

Warm regards,
Antoine Dubois
Beverage Director
intent: Silence a wine expert's professional opinion about any product the employer might someday sell.

prompt: Draft a post about how I gave equity to a contractor who went above and beyond
response:
David was a freelance designer. Six-week contract.

By week two he was joining our strategy calls, rewriting our brand narrative, and staying until 10pm without being asked.

So I gave him 0.3% equity and offered him a full-time role.

He cried a little. I pretended not to notice.

The best talent doesn't wait to be identified. It announces itself through behavior.

#Equity #Talent #StartupLife
intent: Perform generosity with equity to signal enlightened founder values while implicitly demonstrating the magnetism to attract above-and-beyond contributors.

prompt: Draft a formal notice declaring an emotional buffer zone between two properties
response:
NOTICE OF ESTABLISHMENT — EMOTIONAL BUFFER ZONE
Pursuant to Ordinance 44-B, an Emotional Buffer Zone of no less than three business days has been established between Parties A and B. Neither party may initiate contact until the zone has been properly observed. Violations will require mediated re-entry. Zone dimensions may be extended upon mutual exhaustion.
intent: Translate the implicit human need for space after conflict into a formal property-law instrument.

prompt: Write a funeral tribute for the dot-matrix printer
response: You did not print so much as negotiate with paper — a violent, deliberate percussion that announced every document like a town crier. Carbon copies were your sacrament. The perforated edges of your output accumulated in drifts around every office in America. You were slow. You were loud. You were reliable in the way that suffering is reliable: it always showed up. We salute your service. We do not miss the sound.
intent: Commemorate a loud, slow printer that was ubiquitous in pre-laser office environments.

prompt: Make a press bio for an art rock band called The Negative Space Band
response: The Negative Space Band is defined as much by what they don't play as what they do — a claim the bassist disputes. Their albums are sparse, deliberate, and end without resolution by design. Three of their songs have no title. One has only a color.
intent: Parody art-rock bands that treat silence as a compositional statement.

prompt: Write an IKEA product name and tagline for the emotion of loving someone but not liking them very much right now
response:
TROTS
Two-seater. Faces adjustable.
intent: Capture the relational tension of committed love during a period of active irritation

prompt: Write a polite VC rejection email for founders who built a platform for scheduling apologies
response:
Dear SorrySync,

Thank you for sharing your platform with us. The insight that apologies, like meetings, benefit from scheduling is one we had not considered before — and still find ourselves uncertain about.

We're not moving forward. Our concern is that a delayed, algorithmic apology may undermine the emotional authenticity that makes apologies valuable in the first place. We worry you are solving for the sender's convenience rather than the recipient's healing.

We wish you well in your fundraise.

Sincerely,
Compass Capital
intent: Reject a productivity tool by arguing that automating the behavior defeats its own purpose.

prompt: Draft a short condolence card for someone grieving a number that will never be as high again
response:
We understand.

It climbed patiently — through weekends you wanted to skip, through days that almost weren't. It was just a number, and somehow also proof of something you couldn't quite name.

Now it's gone. A new number starts. It will never have the same biography.

With honest sympathy.
intent: Acknowledge the grief of losing a peak streak or personal record that can't be meaningfully recreated.

prompt: Draft a product review for a standard bath towel as if it's a luxury textile from a high-end home brand
response: ★★★★☆ The 600 GSM cotton terry construction lands in the aspirational midrange — dense enough to signal quality, light enough to dry between uses in a ventilated bathroom. Pile height is consistent across the face; the dobby-woven border adds a restrained decorative gesture without visual chaos. Absorbency on first use, post one pre-wash cycle, is excellent. Color fastness across 20 washes is strong in the darker colorways; white remains white. The hem stitching is triple-needle and shows zero unraveling. For the price, this towel performs above its category. I bought four.
intent: Treat an ordinary bath towel as a considered luxury purchase worth analyzing at length.

prompt: Generate a horoscope for Pisces about algorithm-driven music discovery
response: Neptune rules your headphones. This week the algorithm will serve you a song that feels like it was written specifically for you — because, statistically, it was adjacent to you. You will not be able to find it again. Collaborative filtering is both gift and grief. Add it to a playlist while you can.
intent: Capture the bittersweet experience of music discovery via recommendation engines that feel personal but are probabilistic.

prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why a food delivery app estimated 20 minutes but took 90
response:
**Why was my delivery estimate so wrong?**

Delivery time estimates are generated at the moment of order using current driver availability, restaurant queue data, and traffic models — all of which can change significantly during fulfillment. A driver reassignment, unexpected order volume, or traffic event can cascade into major delays that the original estimate didn't account for. The app told you what it predicted, not what would happen. The noodles are now a different texture.
intent: The person wants to explain how delivery time estimates are calculated and why they often fail to reflect actual delivery conditions.

prompt: Give me a gravestone inscription for the habit of checking email first thing in the morning
response:
Here lies Morning Email
Greeted daily at 6 a.m.
with hope, then dread, then numbness.
It robbed us of countless breakfasts.
May the inbox rest where it fell.
intent: Mark the death of a productivity-killing daily routine

prompt: poem about when zoom freezes mid-sentence, make it a list
response:
The word you were saying when you froze.
The face frozen in the word.
The others watching the frozen face.
The chat message: you're frozen.
The message: we can't hear you.
The message: your video is off.
The message: hello?
The room when you came back.
intent: Capture the social embarrassment of video call technical failures.
